EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fog combined with smoke from agricultural burning caused very restricted visibilities along highway US97 about 10 miles south of Klamath Falls.  A jeep rear-ended a car in the fog/smoke at 07:20 AM, and other vehicles collided with them.  In all, 30 vehicles were involved, including three tractor-trailers, along a two mile long stretch of the road.  ||One person, who was driving one of the tractor trailers, was killed.  8 others were hospitalized with mostly minor injuries.  A few others were injured who did not require hospitalization.||At the time, no restrictions to visibility were reported at the nearest ASOS in Klamath Falls (KLMT) or in Alturas (KAAT).   The Montague ASOS (KSIY) reported thick freezing fog at the time, but that station was miles away on the other side of the Cascade range from the collision site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ukiah Complex started August 15th as twenty-nine fires burning on the Umatilla National Forest (UNF) and private lands protected by the Oregon Department of Forestry (ODF). The complex, located 7 miles east of Ukiah, OR, grew rapidly to 4764 acres and was made up of five smaller fires, all caused by lightning.
A human caused wildfire burned 746 acres 5 miles east southeast of Gateway.
The China Diggins Fire burned 157 acres 23 miles east southeast of Long Creek. It burned until August 3.

The Otter Creek Fire located 10 miles east of Dale, Oregon burned 3,032 acres. This fire was one of several fires resulting from a lightning storm that occurred the afternoon of August 15, near Ukiah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two people were pulling crab pots onto a 15 foot boat off Sporthaven Beach near Brookings.  The seas were calm, but suddenly a 12 foot wave arose from the sea.  The boat skipper attempted to outrun the wave, but was swamped.  He made it to the beach but his passenger did not.|     The Coast Guard stated that a new shallow shoal has made this area susceptible to sudden large waves, especially at low tide.|Source:  Medford Mail Tribune newspaper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year brought a blast of cold air to the region along with and following a strong Pacific frontal system.  This brought snow to every area in the county warning area, including heavy amounts of snow to areas along the coast.  Schools all over the region were closed for at least one day, more in places were driving remained perilous.  A minimum of 1 to 4 inches of snow fell over the entire area, with heavier amounts reported on the coast, in the Coast Range and Cascades, and in portions of the Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches accumulated snowfall reported near Eugene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm Pacific frontal system brought widespread steady rain to the forecast area over a period of 36 hours.  This system brought between 2 to 4 inches of rain to the Coast Range, between 1 to 3 inches to the coast and Willamette Valley, and between 2 to 5 inches to the Cascades.  As a result of the steady amounts of rain, a couple rivers within the forecast area reported flooding conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total accumulation of 3.77 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system moving off the Pacific Northwest Coast brought the strongest wind storm in a least a decade to Southern Oregon and Northern California (as well as other areas to the north and south).  A wind gust to 113 mph was recorded at Cape Blanco, which is the highest value recorded there since at least 1998.  Higher gusts may have occurred,  but they were not recorded as a power failure occurred during the latter part of the storm.  There was also an unconfirmed report of a 144 mph gust at Langlois Mountain.  The high winds also generated very high seas and surf.  Excessive rainfall caused some minor small stream flooding.|     Here are the wind-related warnings issued with this storm:  A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022/031 at 01/0400 PST, effective from 02/0600 until 03/2200 PST.  The beginning of the event was moved up to 02/0321 at 02/0321 PST.  The ending of the event was moved up to 03/1700 PST at 03/0909 for ORZ021/022 only.   A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ024/026 at 02/1150 PST, effective from 02/1300 until 02/1900 PST.  It was canceled at 02/1449 PST.  Numerous Wind Advisories were also issued for this event. |    Other warnings issued included a High Surf Warning for Oregon zones ORZ021/022, issued at 01/0159 PST and effective from 02/1000 until 04/1600 PST.  The warning was extended until 04/2100 PST at 03/1200 PST, then it was downgraded to an advisory at 04/2010 PST.  This warning verified.   A Coastal Flood Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022 at 01/1649 PST, effective from 02/1600 PST until 04/1600 PST.  It did not verify and was canceled at 03/1352 PST.   A Flood Warning was issued for the South Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point at 03/1816 PST, effect from 03/2200 until 04/1000 PST.  It was extended until 04/1800 PST at 03/1916 PST, then canceled at 04/0929 PST.  The warning did not verify.   Another Flood Warning was issued for the Coquille River at Coquille at 03/1916 PST, effective from 04/0600 until 05/0600 PST.  It was canceled at 04/0929 PST and did not verify.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recorded at the Squaw Peak Raws.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm Pacific frontal system brought widespread steady rain to the forecast area over a period of 36 hours.  This system brought between 2 to 4 inches of rain to the Coast Range, between 1 to 3 inches to the coast and Willamette Valley, and between 2 to 5 inches to the Cascades.  As a result of the steady amounts of rain, a couple rivers within the forecast area reported flooding conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total accumulation of 1.93 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year brought a blast of cold air to the region along with and following a strong Pacific frontal system.  This brought snow to every area in the county warning area, including heavy amounts of snow to areas along the coast.  Schools all over the region were closed for at least one day, more in places were driving remained perilous.  A minimum of 1 to 4 inches of snow fell over the entire area, with heavier amounts reported on the coast, in the Coast Range and Cascades, and in portions of the Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches accumulated snowfall reported near Nehalem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southwest flow aloft combined with cold air at the surface brought heavy snow to northcentral and northeast oregon. Snowfall amounts in inches included Tollgate (6), 2 miles east of Meacham (7.5), Flora (4), 7 miles south of Lostine (5.5), 11 miles west of Maupin (4), 11 miles northwest of Warm Springs (4.5).

A deep low pressure area moving across British Columbia combined with very strong upper level winds produced damaging surface winds across northeast Oregon.  The winds were most devastating near Wallowa Lake where dozens of trees were downed and crashed into buildings and vehicles. Wallowa Lake State Park was closed as a result of the damage. Wind gusts in miles per hour across the area include Pendleton (62), Pilot Rock (60), Joseph (78), 10 miles south of Imnaha (61), Long Creek (64), 3 miles north of Helix (59). Sustained winds over 40 mph were measured at Hot Lake and North Powder. A wind gust to 111 mph was measured at Sheep Ridge, near Enterprise at an elevation of 7000 feet.

A vigorous Pacific storm system brought heavy snowfall to central Oregon and the Blue Mountains.  Blizzard conditions were observed in the Grande Ronde Valley which closed Interstate 84 for several hours. At the Ladd Canyon rest area visibility frequently was below 1/4 mile with wind gusts 35-53 mph between 10 pm and 6 am.  Snowfall in inches included Tollgate (8), 2 miles east of Meacham (15), LaPine (6.5), 12 miles east northeast of LaPine (12).

A vigorous upper level trough combined with a stationary front laying northeast to southwest across the Blue Mountains produced heavy snow.  Snowfall amounts in inches were Tollgate (11), Meacham (12), 6 miles southwest of Meacham (10), 10 miles north northwest of Ukiah (18), Pendleton NWS (4.8), 3 miles north of Helix (7), 5 miles east of Pine Grove (7), 1 mile west of Wamic (6), Summerville (12), 1 mile north of LaGrande (6.5).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong straight-line winds associated with a thunderstorm swept through portions of Jackson County.  Numerous downed large trees were reported, and some of them damaged vehicles and trailers when they fell.  Most of the damage was reported near the city of Rogue River.  No injuries or deaths were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Freeze Warning was issued for California zone CAZ084 and Oregon Zone ORZ029 at 28/0211 PDT, effective from 29/0100-29/0900 PDT. The warning was cancelled at 29/0900 PST. Reported low temperatures in ORZ029 ranged from 17 to 24 degrees, so this warning verified.
A Frost Advisory was issued for Oregon zone ORZ029 this night...but reported lows ranged from 20 to 30 degrees.  A Freeze Warning should have been issued for this event.

Another in a series of cold, moist Pacific frontal systems brought heavy snow to the Cascades and Cascade Foothills, and to the Upper Hood River Valley, building the steadily increasing mountain snow pack.  Lighter amounts fell in other areas of the forecast area. This frontal system also brought localized strong and gusty winds to the coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 9 inches total snowfall accumulation fell based on similar conditions and reports from the Columbia River Gorge on the Washington side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture combined with strong surface heating made for scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms on this date.  A few of the thunderstorms achieved severe status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Klamath Falls Herald and News reported extensive wind, rain, and hail damage to crops around Malin and the Yonna valleys.  Crop damage included 1000 to 1800 acres of strawberry root stock and several alfalfa and potato fields.  No cost estimate is available at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture combined with strong surface heating made for scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms on this date. A few of the thunderstorms achieved severe status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The steeple of the Newman United Methodist Church was struck twice this day, damaging the steeple, windows, organ, and some other stuff.  Damage amounted to $60,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe and wet thunderstorm fueled by monsoonal moisture struck south Ashland on the afternoon and evening of 08/30/2007.  The severe hail associated with the storm was apparently short-lived and highly localized, but flooding and wind damage was more widespread.  The heaviest rain reports were around 3 inches in 4 hours.  A Flash Flood Warning was issued for this storm for both the Ashland area and north central Siskiyou county, where the storm started.  The first warning for north central Siskiyou county was issued at 30/1745 PDT, effective from 30/1745 PDT until 30/1815 PDT.  It was extended until 30/1845 PDT at 30/1813 PDT.  It expired at 30/1845 PDT.  The second warning for the Ashland area was issued at 30/1853 PDT, effective from 30/1853 PDT until 30/1930 PDT.  It was extended until 30/2000 PDT at 30/1925 PDT.  It expired at 30/2000 PDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported that water in Hamilton creek at the corner of East Main and Clay Creek was one foot from bankfull and rising rapidly.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold air mass was in place over Oregon when a weak disturbance brought some moisture to the area.  Low level snowfall was anticipated with forecast amounts of less than an inch, but the system had more moisture than expected.  2 to 5 inches of snow fell across large areas of southwest Oregon, right down to the coast.  Numerous minor traffic accidents, road closures, and school closures occurred as a result of this storm. |A Snow Advisory was issued for Oregon zones ORZ023/024/026 at 16/1034 PST, effective until 16/1500 PST.  It expired at 16/1500 PST.  Most reported snowfall amounts fell in the 1-3 inch category, but some areas experienced 4 or more inches of snow, which meets Winter Storm Warning criteria in those areas.  Those reports are listed here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter north of Shady Cove reported 4.5 inches of snow during this interval.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Destructive winds hit north central and northeast Oregon as strong winds aloft combined with a tight surface pressure gradient.  Wind gusts in mph included 4 miles southeast of Imbler (59), 3 miles north of Joseph (84), Enterprise (58), Ladd Canyon Summit (58), Pilot Rock (66), North Pole Ridge RAWS (59). Wind damage reports came from Union, 8 miles southeast of Pilot Rock, 9 miles east northeast of Lexington, 6 miles east southeast of Pendleton, and Madras.  Sustained winds of greater than 40 mph were observed at Heppner. Interstate 84 was closed on December 2 from 2 pm to 9:30 pm between Pendleton and Ontario. North Powder school was closed on December 3. High winds snapped a power line north of Cove.

Wind gusts in mph included 4 miles southeast of Imbler (59), 3 miles north of Joseph (84), Enterprise (58), Ladd Canyon Summit (58), Pilot Rock (66), North Pole Ridge RAWS (59). Wind damage reports came from Union, 8 miles southeast of Pilot Rock, 9 miles east northeast of Lexington, 6 miles east southeast of Pendleton, and Madras.  Sustained winds of greater than 40 mph were observed at Heppner. Interstate 84 was closed on December 2 from 2 pm to 9:30 pm between Pendleton and Ontario. North Powder school was closed on December 3. High winds snapped a power line north of Cove.

A Frost Advisory was issued for Oregon coastal zones ORZ021/022 for the morning of 12/09/07, but it appears that freezing temperatures were widespread in zone ORZ021.  ORZ022 had a frost event though.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures in zone ORZ021 ranged from 28 to 32 degrees.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL COAST RANGE OF W OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful pacific storm brought very strong winds to portions of northwest Oregon.  Many locations along the coast saw wind gusts in excess of 80 mph, while areas inland saw less severe, however still damaging, winds.  Power outages were sustained in multiple communities across the area. At it's peak, approximately 35000 Oregon customers were without power due to this storm. Many power outages were due to downed trees or tree limbs knocking down power lines.  This also caused multiple highways from the coast to the interior to be closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were reported at the Rockhouse RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Frost Advisory was issued for Oregon coastal zones ORZ021/022 for the morning of 12/09/07, but it appears that freezing temperatures were widespread in zone ORZ021.  ORZ022 had a frost event though.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures in zone ORZ022 ranged from 33 to 41 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a Pacific low pressure storm, cold air was entrenched over northwest Oregon.  As a result, post-frontal unstable shower activity resulted in additional snow accumulations over the lower elevations.  Schools in the greater Portland metro area were closed for a total of two-and-a-half days due to somewhat hazardous traveling conditions on smaller roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of new snow fell in the Portland metro area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Frost Advisory was issued for Oregon coastal zones ORZ021 for the morning of 12/11/07, but it appears that freezing temperatures were widespread in zone ORZ021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures ranged from 27 to 36 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm Pacific frontal system brought widespread steady rain to the forecast area over a period of 36 hours.  This system brought between 2 to 4 inches of rain to the Coast Range, between 1 to 3 inches to the coast and Willamette Valley, and between 2 to 5 inches to the Cascades.  As a result of the steady amounts of rain, a couple rivers within the forecast area reported flooding conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total accumulation of 1.52 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another cold front brought heavy snow to Oregon.  This time the snow fell at unusually low elevations. Snow Advisories were in effect for this event, but no warnings were issued. Some locations did report snow amounts meeting Heavy Snow Warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter N of Glendale reported 4 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 26/0940 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved through the area, producing strong, gusty winds as well as dime to nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported in Beaverton near Cooper Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific storm moved into Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho and stalled over the area producing significant snow over parts of Harney and Malheur counties in Oregon and Southern Twin Falls Zone in Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell near French Glen and twelve inches around the Fields area. Gusty winds caused localized white out conditions during the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell around the Juntura area along Highway 20 to the Harney County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very powerful storms with origins in the Tropics brought hazardous weather to the Pacific Northwest. The entire forecast area experienced heavy rainfall for an extended period of time. As an example of the extreme accumulations, over fourteen inches of rain fell at Lees Camp in the north Coast Range in a period of about 60 hours. This abnormally long period of consistently heavy rainfall led to widespread flooding, with the worst hit areas in the Coast Range and areas draining from the Coast Range to the Pacific Ocean. Five rivers in northwest Oregon surpassed major flood stages, fueling the extensive flood damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Nehalem River flooded near Vernonia, and did devastating damage to the city. Numerous homes were completely flooded, and many families were evacuated. Quite a few lost everything they had. NOTE: Damage estimates are for the entire Columbia county, with almost a third of the damage in the town of Vernonia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trask River near Tillamook reached major flood levels. This, combined with major flooding of two other rivers, led to widespread flooding of the city of Tillamook. Many homes and businesses sustained flood damage as a result. Also, the Port of Tillamook railroad sustained substantial damage due to flooding carrying away portions of the ground and bridges that the railway crosses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm and associated cold front brought strong winds to the coast and heavy snow to the Cascades.  Behind the front in the unstable airmass, snow levels dropped and cold air hung over the forecast area.  Three car crashes were reported, one due to hail on the road, and two due to slick roads, which resulted in four indirect fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold, unstable airmass settled over the area in the wake of a cold frontal passage.  An automobile accident occurred on icy roadways when a  hailstorm covered roadways with a few inches of hail in the Brownsville area.  The accident occurred after a car slid into another lane and collided with a logging semi-truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm and associated cold front brought strong winds to the coast and heavy snow to the Cascades.  Behind the front in the unstable airmass, snow levels dropped and cold air hung over the forecast area.  Three car crashes were reported, one due to hail on the road, and two due to slick roads, which resulted in four indirect fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold air over the area resulting from a recent frontal passage left wet roads susceptible to ice formation overnight. A collision occurred in the morning hours near Salem and was attributed to icy road conditions, which resulted in one indirect fatality.  The accident occurred after a car slid into the path of an oncoming truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very powerful storms with origins in the Tropics brought hazardous weather to the Pacific Northwest. Each storm moved over the coastal Pacific waters in quick succession, and were unusually strong. With the stronger winds, seas built to heights of 44 to 48 feet. The seas combined with high tides caused widespread coastal flooding. Highest tides occurred Monday morning, December 3rd, and storm surges were between 2 and 5 feet over the predicted tide height. Also, as a result of the unusually high seas, both near shore buoys were lost in the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding occurred along the coastline south of Lincoln City. Some minor damage to property and roads were reported. NOTE: Damage estimates are for the entire Central Oregon Coast, but mainly for areas in Lincoln county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture left over from rains the weekend before in combination with areas of fog forming in areas of below freezing temperatures led to the formation of black ice on roadways throughout much of the forecast area. Two casualties were reported after vehicle accidents occured, and were reported to be caused by the black ice conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was traveling on Highway 30 en route from Hillsboro to Warren, and lost control of the vehicle.  The accident was attributed to icy conditions on the roadway at the time of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong combination of storms brought an extended period of rainfall to northwest Oregon. Accumulations from this recent rainfall in the north Oregon Coast Range were around a foot or more of water, which fell in a relatively short period of time--just over two days. The heavy rainfall, in combination with steep sloped terrain caused a debris flow of rare magnitude. The landslide reportedly traveled at a speed of 30 to 45 mph. This slide engulfed homes in its path and even carried one down with it. The landslide washed over Highway 30, closing the highway to all traffic for about four days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A massive debris flow, 10 feet deep and 900 yards wide, holding approximately 40,000 cubic yards of mud and debris, swept over Highway 30 about 8 miles west of Clatskanie only 90 minutes after homes in the area were evacuated. Four homes, several automobiles, boats, and an RV were destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A family was traveling on Highway 35 south of Odell when they hit an icy patch in a shady spot on the road, lost control of the vehicle, and spun into oncoming traffic. Five people were hurt in the accident. Two of those people, a mother and daughter, died of their injuries later.

The entire forecast area experienced heavy rainfall for an extended period of time. As an example of the extreme accumulations, over fourteen inches of rain fell at Lees Camp in the north Coast Range in a period of about 60 hours. This abnormally long period of consistently heavy rainfall led to widespread flooding, with the worst hit areas in the Coast Range and areas draining from the Coast Range to the Pacific Ocean. Five rivers in northwest Oregon surpassed major flood stages, fueling the extensive flood damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total precipitation was 7.30 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very powerful storms with origins in the Tropics brought hazardous weather to the Pacific Northwest. Each storm moved over the coastal Pacific waters in quick succession, and were unusually strong. With the stronger winds, seas built to heights of 44 to 48 feet. The seas combined with high tides caused widespread coastal flooding. Highest tides occurred Monday morning, December 3rd, and storm surges were between 2 and 5 feet over the predicted tide height. Also, as a result of the unusually high seas, both near shore buoys were lost in the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread coastal flooding was occurring along the coastline around Tillamook Bay, and hundreds of people were evacuated from the area. NOTE: Damage estimate is for the entire North Oregon Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm Pacific frontal system brought widespread steady rain to the forecast area over a period of 36 hours.  This system brought between 2 to 4 inches of rain to the Coast Range, between 1 to 3 inches to the coast and Willamette Valley, and between 2 to 5 inches to the Cascades.  As a result of the steady amounts of rain, a couple rivers within the forecast area reported flooding conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clackamas River near Estacada crested at 20.2 feet on January 3rd. Flood stage for this river is 20.0 feet.

A Freeze Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022 at 10/0333 PST, effective from 10/2200 PST through 12/0800 PST (two nights). It was later extended until 13/1000 PST at 10/1444 PST (three nights), to 14/1000 PST at 12/0703 (four nights), and finally to 15/1000 at 13/0315 PST (five nights).  Reported low temperatures on the morning of 01/15 ranged from 23 to 37 degrees, so freezing temperatures were widespread in the warning area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe and wet thunderstorm fueled by monsoonal moisture struck south Ashland on the afternoon and evening of 08/30/2007.  The severe hail associated with the storm was apparently short-lived and highly localized, but flooding and wind damage was more widespread.  The heaviest rain reports were around 3 inches in 4 hours.  A Flash Flood Warning was issued for this storm for both the Ashland area and north central Siskiyou county, where the storm started.  The first warning for north central Siskiyou county was issued at 30/1745 PDT, effective from 30/1745 PDT until 30/1815 PDT.  It was extended until 30/1845 PDT at 30/1813 PDT.  It expired at 30/1845 PDT.  The second warning for the Ashland area was issued at 30/1853 PDT, effective from 30/1853 PDT until 30/1930 PDT.  It was extended until 30/2000 PDT at 30/1925 PDT.  It expired at 30/2000 PDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in South Ashland reported one foot of water running over the intersection of Whiteman and Siskiyou.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very strong storms brought hazardous winter weather conditions to the Pacific northwest.  The first of these storms rocked the coast of north and central Oregon with very strong winds. The storm approached from the west-southwest overnight Saturday night, December 1st, and the frontal system reached the coastline by early Sunday, December 2nd. The strongest winds were experienced Sunday afternoon. The high wind speeds associated with this storm brought down not only tree limbs, but whole trees. Multiple roads were closed along the coast and up into the coast range due to downed trees and power lines. Also, power was lost to the entire coastline between Yachats and Pacific City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust recorded in Bay City. This was the highest wind gust report of the entire event. Other impressive wind reports include: gusts to 90 kt at Rockaway Beach; gusts to 75 kt in Astoria as well as on Clatsop Spit; 52 kt with gusts to 74 kt at the Astoria airport; gusts to 65 kt in Tillamook as well as gusts to 64 kt at the Tillamook airport.||Other wind gusts were also recorded on Dec 2 on Clatsop Spit, west of Astoria. Sustained winds of 59 kt were also recorded. Other wind reports include: gusts to 70 kt at both Cannon Beach and Youngs Bay; gusts to 67 kt at Rockaway Beach;; gusts to 54 kt at Tillamook Bay; gusts to 53 kt at Garibaldi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very strong storms brought hazardous winter weather conditions to the Pacific Northwest. The second and more powerful of these storms rocked the coast of north and central Oregon with hurricane force winds, and brought strong winds to the Coast Range, Cascades, and Cascade Foothills as well. The second storm closely followed the first storm.  The frontal system associated with the second storm moved over the coastline Sunday night, December 2nd into Monday, December 3rd. The strongest winds were experienced in the early morning hours Monday, December 3rd. The high wind speeds associated with this storm caused widespread damage to the the area. The hardest hit counties were Clatsop, Columbia, and Tillamook counties.  Most communities in these counties were without power and communications for four days following the event.  All east-west roads leading from the Willamette Valley to the north and central Oregon Coast were closed due to downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust recorded at the White River Pass, along Highway 35. Gusts to 56 kt were also recorded at the Mount Hood Meadows ski resort.

Multiple roads were closed along the coast and up into the coast range due to downed trees and power lines. Also, power was lost to the entire coastline between Yachats and Pacific City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust recorded at the Rockhouse RAWS, about 20 miles west of Dallas. NOTE: Damage estimate is for entire Central Oregon Coast Range.  Other winds were reported at the Cedar RAWS site.  Also, reports were given of wind-downed trees which closed Hwy. 20 for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds across the Medford CWA from the evening of 10/18/07 through about noon on 10/19/07.  A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022 at 18/1419 PDT, effective from 18/2000 until 19/0500 PDT.  It expired at 19/0500 PDT.  More High Wind Warnings were issued at 19/0335 PDT.  The one for Oregon zones ORZ027/028 and California zone CAZ083 was effective from 19/0335 PDT until 19/0900 PDT.  It expired at 19/0900 PDT.  One for Oregon zone ORZ031 was effective from 19/0335 PDT until 19/1100 PDT.  It expired at 19/1100 PDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on observations at representative stations in zone ORZ028, such as Squaw Peak (gust to 83 mph), similar winds likely occurred in ORZ027.  A buoy on Crater Lake recorded a gust to 55 mph at 0300 PDT on 10/19, and this buoy is well below the rim, where winds were likely much higher.  Based on these observations, it is assumed that this warning verified in zone ORZ027.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds across the Medford CWA from the evening of 10/18/07 through about noon on 10/19/07.  A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022 at 18/1419 PDT, effective from 18/2000 until 19/0500 PDT.  It expired at 19/0500 PDT.  More High Wind Warnings were issued at 19/0335 PDT. One for Oregon zone ORZ031 was effective from 19/0335 PDT until 19/1100 PDT.  It expired at 19/1100 PDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on observations at nearby locations, particularly at Gold Beach (gusts to 60 mph) and Cape Arago (gusts to 71 mph) and Cape Blanco (gusts to 74 mph), it is assumed that this High Wind Warning verified in ORZ021 during this interval.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very strong storms brought hazardous winter weather conditions to the Pacific Northwest. The second and more powerful of these storms rocked the coast of north and central Oregon with hurricane force winds, and brought strong winds to the Coast Range, Cascades, and Cascade Foothills as well. The second storm closely followed the first storm.  The frontal system associated with the second storm moved over the coastline Sunday night, December 2nd into Monday, December 3rd. The strongest winds were experienced in the early morning hours Monday, December 3rd. The high wind speeds associated with this storm caused widespread damage to the the area. The hardest hit counties were Clatsop, Columbia, and Tillamook counties.  Most communities in these counties were without power and communications for four days following the event.  All east-west roads leading from the Willamette Valley to the north and central Oregon Coast were closed due to downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust recorded at the Brush Creek RAWS. Gusts to 53 kt were also recorded in Marcola, located about 10 miles northeast of the Eugene/Springfield area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening under an unstable airmass, thunderstorms were prevalent over much of northwest Oregon. One particular storm grew to such strength to produce dime to nickel sized hail and wind gusts up to 50 mph.
During an afternoon under an unstable airmass, small thunderstorms moved across northwest Oregon. One storm produced a small hail storm, with enough hail accumulation to cover the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground near the Gateway Transit Center, with biggest stones being half an inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under an unstable airmass, low-topped thunderstorms dominated northwest Oregon. One such storm produced heavy amounts of small hail, one-half inch in diameter - enough to accumulate a few inches on the ground before the storm moved away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported residents estimated half an inch accumulations on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first wind event of the season brought strong winds and minor damage to the coast and Coast Range of northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This occurred at Cape Mears, which also measured sustained winds of 43 knots. This wind gust observations was the last recorded before the wind equipment failed. Other wind reports received include: 47 kt with gusts to 69 kt at Garibaldi; gusts to 63 kt at the Yaquina Bay Bridge; gusts to 52 kt at Seaside; gusts to 57 kt at Rockaway Beach; gusts to 60 kt at Cape Disappointment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but powerful system brought strong winds to a few areas along the north Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were reported at Garibaldi, and recorded sustained wind speeds of 59 kt. Sustained winds of 38 kt were also reported at Clatsop Spit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm and associated cold front brought strong winds to the coast and heavy snow to the Cascades.  Behind the front in the unstable airmass, snow levels dropped and cold air hung over the forecast area.  Three car crashes were reported, one due to hail on the road, and two due to slick roads, which resulted in four indirect fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust reported at Clatsop Spit. Sustained winds of 45 kt were also reported. The Astoria airport reported gusts to 54 kt as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm and associated cold front brought strong winds to the coast and heavy snow to the Cascades. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust reported in Lincoln City. Other wind reports include: 42 kt with gusts to 52 kt at the Newport airport; 40 kt with gusts to 53 kt in Florence; gusts to 63 kt on the Yaquina Bay Bridge along Hwy. 101.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but potent Pacific low pressure system brought strong winds to the coast of Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds reported on the Hwy. 101 bridge over Yaquina Bay, as well as similar winds in Florence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe and wet thunderstorm fueled by monsoonal moisture struck south Ashland on the afternoon and evening of 08/30/2007.  The severe hail associated with the storm was apparently short-lived and highly localized, but flooding and wind damage was more widespread.  The heaviest rain reports were around 3 inches in 4 hours.  A Flash Flood Warning was issued for this storm for both the Ashland area and north central Siskiyou county, where the storm started.  The first warning for north central Siskiyou county was issued at 30/1745 PDT, effective from 30/1745 PDT until 30/1815 PDT.  It was extended until 30/1845 PDT at 30/1813 PDT.  It expired at 30/1845 PDT.  The second warning for the Ashland area was issued at 30/1853 PDT, effective from 30/1853 PDT until 30/1930 PDT.  It was extended until 30/2000 PDT at 30/1925 PDT.  It expired at 30/2000 PDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A city of Ashland emergency manager reported numerous trees down around the city, including one that was 4 feet in diameter that fell through a house.  Two large trees in Garfield Park were also down.  The fire department reported 12 calls for downed power lines, but it is not known how many of these calls were for the same line.

Another in a series of cold, moist Pacific frontal systems brought heavy snow to the Cascades and Cascade Foothills, and to the Upper Hood River Valley, building the steadily increasing mountain snow pack. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 to 9 inches total snowfall accumulation fell, based on spotter reports from spotters in Parkdale, Dee, and Odell.

Ahead of a round of devastating storms in the Pacific Northwest, snow levels were very low, down to the valley floor in some regions.  Precipitation fell as snow over the North Oregon Cascades for an extended period of time before the much warmer systems moved in and changed all the precipitation to rain. Also, freezing rain fell for a time in the Columbia River Gorge during the transition to a warmer air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 1 inch of sleet and freezing rain fell on top of 2 to 3 inches of snow in the Hood River area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful pacific storm brought very strong winds to portions of northwest Oregon.  Many locations along the coast saw wind gusts in excess of 80 mph, while areas inland saw less severe, however still damaging, winds.  Power outages were sustained in multiple communities across the area. At it's peak, approximately 35000 Oregon customers were without power due to this storm. Many power outages were due to downed trees or tree limbs knocking down power lines.  This also caused multiple highways from the coast to the interior to be closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were reported at Clatsop Spit, which also recorded sustained wind speeds of 63 kt. Other wind reports with this system include: 55 kt with gusts to 72 kt at Garibaldi; 36 kt with gusts to 55 kt at the Astoria airport; gusts to 63 kt at Cannon Beach.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 14 to 25 inches total accumulated snowfall fell, based on reports from Bennett Pass along Hwy. 35, Timberline Lodge and Mount Hood Meadows ski resorts on Mt. Hood, and Blue Box Pass along Hwy. 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden Pacific storm brought healthy amounts of snowfall to the Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 9 and 20 inches accumulated snowfall was reported, based on observations from the Mount Hood Meadows and Timberline ski resorts, Government Camp ODOT station along Hwy. 26, and Santiam Pass Summit ODOT station along Hwy. 20.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amounts of 8 to 20 inches total accumulated snowfall fell, based on reports from within the Bull Run Reservoir along the Sandy River as well as from Zig Zag along Hwy. 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of cold, moist Pacific frontal systems brought heavy snow to the Cascades and Cascade Foothills, and to the Upper Hood River Valley, building the steadily increasing mountain snow pack.  Lighter amounts fell in other areas of the forecast area. This frontal system also brought localized strong and gusty winds to the coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recorded on the Yaquina Bay Bridge along Hwy. 101 just south of Newport. Sustained winds of 42 kt were also reported. Sustained winds of 42 kt with gusts to 50 kt were also reported in Florence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another in a series of cold, moist Pacific frontal systems brought heavy snow to the Cascades. Westerly flow behind the advancing cold front orographically enhanced snowfall potential for this storm. This storm was the fourth storm in six days to bring heavy snow to the Cascades, continuing to build the steadily increasing mountain snow pack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 18 and 24 inches total snowfall accumulation was recorded, based on reports from the Mount Hood Meadows and Timberline ski resorts on Mt. Hood, and from the Santiam Pass Summit ODOT reporting station on Hwy. 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system moving off the Pacific Northwest Coast brought the strongest wind storm in a least a decade to Southern Oregon and Northern California (as well as other areas to the north and south).  A wind gust to 113 mph was recorded at Cape Blanco, which is the highest value recorded there since at least 1998.  Higher gusts may have occurred,  but they were not recorded as a power failure occurred during the latter part of the storm.  There was also an unconfirmed report of a 144 mph gust at Langlois Mountain.  The high winds also generated very high seas and surf.  Excessive rainfall caused some minor small stream flooding.|     Here are the wind-related warnings issued with this storm:  A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022/031 at 01/0400 PST, effective from 02/0600 until 03/2200 PST.  The beginning of the event was moved up to 02/0321 at 02/0321 PST.  The ending of the event was moved up to 03/1700 PST at 03/0909 for ORZ021/022 only.   A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ024/026 at 02/1150 PST, effective from 02/1300 until 02/1900 PST.  It was canceled at 02/1449 PST.  Numerous Wind Advisories were also issued for this event. |    Other warnings issued included a High Surf Warning for Oregon zones ORZ021/022, issued at 01/0159 PST and effective from 02/1000 until 04/1600 PST.  The warning was extended until 04/2100 PST at 03/1200 PST, then it was downgraded to an advisory at 04/2010 PST.  This warning verified.   A Coastal Flood Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022 at 01/1649 PST, effective from 02/1600 PST until 04/1600 PST.  It did not verify and was canceled at 03/1352 PST.   A Flood Warning was issued for the South Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point at 03/1816 PST, effect from 03/2200 until 04/1000 PST.  It was extended until 04/1800 PST at 03/1916 PST, then canceled at 04/0929 PST.  The warning did not verify.   Another Flood Warning was issued for the Coquille River at Coquille at 03/1916 PST, effective from 04/0600 until 05/0600 PST.  It was canceled at 04/0929 PST and did not verify.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Normally the Cape Blanco observation is not used to verify High Wind Warnings due to the extreme windiness of the site.  However, the magnitude of the event produced extraordinary observations here.   The peak gust was 113 mph, recorded at 2225 PST on 12/02.  Cape Blanco exceeded High Wind Warning criteria continuously from 0110 PST on 12/02 until power failed at the site after the 1040 PST observation on 12/04, a period of 2 days, 9 hours, and 30 minutes.  Warning criteria was likely met at the site through at least 1800 PST on 12/04.||Other observations in ORZ021 that met Warning criteria (all times PST).|02/0508-0708 Bald Knob RAWS exceeded 60 mph continuously during this|     interval.  Peak gust 70 mph at 02/0608.  |02/0508-03/1713 Long Prairie RAWS met criteria in 30 out of 34|     observations.  Peak gust 83 mph at 02/1113.|02/0823 Spotter 1W Bandon reported a gust to 52 KT.|02/1459 Spotter N Langlois reported a gust to 58 KT.|02/1710 Spotter 1W Bandon reported a gust to 66 KT.|03/0012 Spotter N Langlois reported a gust to 61 KT with gusts in the|     60s mph most hours of the day.|03/0508 Bald Knob RAWS reported a gust to 58 KT.|03/0515 Bandon recorded a gust to 60 KT.|03/0530 Spotter 1W Bandon reported a gust to 64 KT with lots of gusts|     in the 60s mph.|03/1320 Estimated gusts to 52 KT.  A Charleston spotter reported|     shredded sails, dumpsters overturned, and shingles off roofs.|03/1345 Estimated gusts to 65 mph.  A spotter 15S North Bend reported|     tree debris flying through the air and partial roof loss on mobile |     home.|03/1347 Estimated gusts to 60 KT. A spotter N Langlois reported |     branches down on power lines, uprooted trees, and power outages.|03/1355-1455 The North Bend airport recorded gusts to 58, 59, and 66 mph|     during this interval.|03/1413-1513 The Seven Mile Creek RAWS recorded gusts to 60 and 69 mph|     during this interval.|03/1625 The Umpqua Discovery Center reported a gust to 80 mph, but the |     time of the gust was not recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved onshore during this interval...bringing high winds to the coast and to the typically windiest inland locations.  A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022 at 17/0345 PST, effective from 17/2200 til 18/0700 PST.  It was extended until 18/1000 PST at 18/0346 PST.  It was cancelled at 18/0946 PST.  The warning verified at several locations in the warning area.  High winds were also reported well inland at Summer Lake, which typically gets localized high winds in this pattern.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS recorded gusts of 58 mph or greater continuously during an interval from 18/0038 until 18/0938, and again from 18/1438 until 18/1538 PST.  The peak gust was 71 mph at 18/0838 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another incoming cold front brought a minimal High Wind event to the coast and to isolated inland areas.   No warnings were issued for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gold Beach recorded gusts meeting or exceeding High Wind Warning criteria in 10 out of 14 observations between 19/0645 and 19/1430 PST.  The peak gust was 74 mph recorded at 19/1145 PST.  Humbug Mountain (ODOT58) recorded a gust to 59 KT at 19/0604 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another cold front brought heavy snow to Oregon.  This time the snow fell at unusually low elevations. Snow Advisories were in effect for this event, but no warnings were issued. Some locations did report snow amounts meeting Heavy Snow Warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Toketee Falls at 2060 feet reported 15.5 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 26/1100 PST. The next day he reported 9.5 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 27/1100 PST, for a total of 25 inches of snow in 48 hours.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN & EASTERN KLAMATH COUNTY & WESTERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system moving off the Pacific Northwest Coast brought the strongest wind storm in a least a decade to Southern Oregon and Northern California (as well as other areas to the north and south).  A wind gust to 113 mph was recorded at Cape Blanco, which is the highest value recorded there since at least 1998.  Higher gusts may have occurred,  but they were not recorded as a power failure occurred during the latter part of the storm.  There was also an unconfirmed report of a 144 mph gust at Langlois Mountain.  The high winds also generated very high seas and surf.  Excessive rainfall caused some minor small stream flooding.|     Here are the wind-related warnings issued with this storm:  A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022/031 at 01/0400 PST, effective from 02/0600 until 03/2200 PST.  The beginning of the event was moved up to 02/0321 at 02/0321 PST.  The ending of the event was moved up to 03/1700 PST at 03/0909 for ORZ021/022 only.   A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ024/026 at 02/1150 PST, effective from 02/1300 until 02/1900 PST.  It was canceled at 02/1449 PST.  Numerous Wind Advisories were also issued for this event. |    Other warnings issued included a High Surf Warning for Oregon zones ORZ021/022, issued at 01/0159 PST and effective from 02/1000 until 04/1600 PST.  The warning was extended until 04/2100 PST at 03/1200 PST, then it was downgraded to an advisory at 04/2010 PST.  This warning verified.   A Coastal Flood Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022 at 01/1649 PST, effective from 02/1600 PST until 04/1600 PST.  It did not verify and was canceled at 03/1352 PST.   A Flood Warning was issued for the South Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point at 03/1816 PST, effect from 03/2200 until 04/1000 PST.  It was extended until 04/1800 PST at 03/1916 PST, then canceled at 04/0929 PST.  The warning did not verify.   Another Flood Warning was issued for the Coquille River at Coquille at 03/1916 PST, effective from 04/0600 until 05/0600 PST.  It was canceled at 04/0929 PST and did not verify.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the peak wind recorded at the Calimus RAWS.  Other gusts meeting warning criteria (all times PST):  52 KT at 02/1800, 50 KT at 03/0000, and 51 KT at 03/0200.  It should be noted that the Calimus RAWS is at a high altitude and frequently records stronger winds than other nearby stations.  These wind values are not indicative of winds in the valleys, where most of the population lives.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another front brought high winds to some areas of the Southern Oregon Coast this day.  No warnings were issued for this system.  Two stations recorded high winds:  Gold Beach and the US101 at Humbug Mountain.

It was cancelled at 18/0946 PST.  The warning verified at several locations in the warning area.  High winds were also reported well inland at Summer Lake, which typically gets localized high winds in this pattern.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought high winds to the area...especially along the coast.   A High Wind Warning was issued for Oregon zones ORZ021/022 at 16/1501 PDT, effective from 18/0500 PDT until 18/1400 PDT.  The warning expired at 18/1400 PDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sensor at Port Orford recorded winds gusts verifying a High Wind Warning from 18/0706 until 18/1724 PDT.  A gust to 52 KT was recorded at 07:06 PDT, followed by gusts to 53 KT at 07:18 and to 52 KT at 07:24.  A spotter 1SE Bandon recorded a gust to 51 KT at 18/0651 PDT.  At 18/0351, a spotter north of Langlois reported that the wind was blowing hard and knocking trees down.  Wind gusts to 50 KT were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved through the area, producing strong, gusty winds as well as dime to nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports received of strong wind resulting trees blown down in downtown Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three teenage swimmers were swept away from the beach of Cannon Beach, OR by a rip current. Two swimmers were able to escape the current, but one was swept into the sea. Cannon Beach's Surf Rescue Team along with Cannon Bach Life Guards were the first to respond. The Coast Guard was called, and searchers searched through the night and into the morning. The search for the missing teenager was called off the next morning.  The body was found about one week later when he washed ashore approximately 20 miles south of Cannon Beach.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL COAST RANGE OF W OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first wind event of the season brought strong winds and minor damage to the coast and Coast Range of northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This occurred at the Rockhouse RAWS, located around 4 miles north-northwest of Falls City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first wind event of the season brought strong winds and minor damage to the coast and Coast Range of northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of wind-downed trees knocking down power lines along Hwy. 202 are sufficient for estimated wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds with gusts 40-50 mph which knocked down trees and power lines in Sisters. One tree fell onto a house.

Two very powerful storms with origins in the Tropics brought hazardous weather to the Pacific Northwest. The entire forecast area experienced heavy rainfall for an extended period of time. As an example of the extreme accumulations, over fourteen inches of rain fell at Lees Camp in the north Coast Range in a period of about 60 hours. This abnormally long period of consistently heavy rainfall led to widespread flooding, with the worst hit areas in the Coast Range and areas draining from the Coast Range to the Pacific Ocean. Five rivers in northwest Oregon surpassed major flood stages, fueling the extensive flood damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nehalem River at Foss reached major flood levels. There was widespread flooding and damage in the city of Nehalem, and a trailer park was evacuated. Also along the Nehalem River, a 54-year old woman drove her truck into flooded waters and was swept away.  The body was never found, and the woman was presumed dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Trout Meadows Fire, 9 miles northwest of Granite, was started by lightning. It burned 3,900 acres of primarily lodgepole pine and grass from August 3 to August 20.
The 621 Fire was human caused and burned 15,600 acres of mainly grass and shrubs 13 miles east of Moro.

The GW Fire, 13 miles west of Sisters, was started by lightning and burned 7,357 acres between August 31 and September 11.  Residents of Black Butte Ranch were evacuated on September 3 and allowed to return September 4.

Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front.  Afternoon heating of a moist and unstable atmosphere combined with an active jet stream aloft sustained strong to severe thunderstorms with abundant lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds broke tree limbs 6 inches in diameter across power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area in southwest Oregon produced numerous thunderstorms across Central Oregon and Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 0.65 inch of rainfall in 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Saddle Gulch Fire, 4 miles east southeast of Imnaha, was started by lightning and burned 4100 acres from September 16 to 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire burned 2700 acres, 5 outbuildings, and 1 residence, 9 miles southeast of Fossil. The fire also burned grass, brush, and timberlands in steep and difficult terrain.

In an unstable atmosphere, several thunderstorms moved over the forecast area. One such storm produced a short-lived tornado, that did considerable damage to a local farm.  Dime size hail was also reported with the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm survey found tornado off of Tennessee School Drive was on the ground for 5-10 minutes, with a path a little less than a mile in length, and maximum width 350 yards. Tornado tracked northwest to southeast over open fields and then in trees. Six farm buildings damaged, mostly roof damage. Estimated 90-100 mature trees damaged, with 20-30 of them snapped off low in the trunk, and 6 to 8 uprooted. One tree fell on farmhouse sustaining minor damage. Rated EF0 on enhanced Fujita scale, with gusts 75 to 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre-frontal pressure gradients and an upper level low off the Pacific Coast combined to produce damaging winds during the early evening. Trees and power lines were downed north northwest of LaPine and 4 miles southwest of Sunriver.

A strong offshore pressure gradient brought strong easterly winds to higher elevations around Crater Lake National Park down to the Prospect area.  Numerous trees were knocked over, some with trunk diameters of four feet.  Most of  this damage occurred above the 4000 foot elevation line, and several forest roads and State Highway 62 were briefly closed due to fallen trees blocking the road.  No injuries were reported and there was some anecdotal talk of damage to trailers, but none of those reports were confirmed.  The one confirmed damage report was that of a car damaged by a falling tree near Crater Lake National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported near Crater Lake National Park. Two dozen mature hemlocks down, some uprooted and some snapped off.  Diameters ranged from 18 inches to 3-4 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient across the Grande Ronde Valley set up locally very strong winds. Sustained winds of 49 mph near Ladd Canyon and 45 mph at Cove were reported.

A strong warm Pacific frontal system brought widespread steady rain to the forecast area over a period of 36 hours.  This system brought between 2 to 4 inches of rain to the Coast Range, between 1 to 3 inches to the coast and Willamette Valley, and between 2 to 5 inches to the Cascades.  As a result of the steady amounts of rain, a couple rivers within the forecast area reported flooding conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total accumulation of 1.52 inches was reported.
